coredata.dc:description: Acrylic resins are widely used in the marine industry due to their low cost, weathering and pollution resistance, but deficiencies in corrosion resistance and poor stability limit their applications. To overcome this challenge, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with special structures (Pyridine, Pyrrole and Graphite N’s) and multiple functional groups were successfully synthesized in this study using citric acid and ethylenediamine as the precursors, with the former special structure displaying a key role in catalyzing the conversion of corrosive medium (O2), and the latter being able to form a stable complex with Fe3+, which effectively retarded the corrosion occurrence. Moreover, the nitrogen, hydrogen, and oxygen atoms in the N-CQDs can form hydrogen-bonded cross-links with the acrylic resin, which not only enhances the self-healing ability of the acrylic resin, but also it significantly improves the overall stability. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis indicated that the impedance modulus of the N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coatings prepared after the introduction of N-CQDs into PAZ/N-PMI resins was as high as 1011 Ω∙cm2, which was four orders of magnitude higher than that of the pure PAZ/N-PMI coatings (107 Ω∙cm2). Furthermore, the coating remained effective after 59 days of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, demonstrating its long-term corrosion protection. Further microscopic characterization results demonstrated that the N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coatings have excellent self-healing ability compared with the pure resin coatings.

head.ce:title: Study on anticorrosive and self-healing properties of acrylic resin modified with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Acrylic resins are widely used in the marine industry due to their low cost, weathering and pollution resistance, but deficiencies in corrosion resistance and poor stability limit their applications. To overcome this challenge, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with special structures (Pyridine, Pyrrole and Graphite N’s) and multiple functional groups were successfully synthesized in this study using citric acid and ethylenediamine as the precursors, with the former special structure displaying a key role in catalyzing the conversion of corrosive medium (O2), and the latter being able to form a stable complex with Fe3+, which effectively retarded the corrosion occurrence. Moreover, the nitrogen, hydrogen, and oxygen atoms in the N-CQDs can form hydrogen-bonded cross-links with the acrylic resin, which not only enhances the self-healing ability of the acrylic resin, but also it significantly improves the overall stability. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis indicated that the impedance modulus of the N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coatings prepared after the introduction of N-CQDs into PAZ/N-PMI resins was as high as 1011 Ω∙cm2, which was four orders of magnitude higher than that of the pure PAZ/N-PMI coatings (107 Ω∙cm2). Furthermore, the coating remained effective after 59 days of immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, demonstrating its long-term corrosion protection. Further microscopic characterization results demonstrated that the N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coatings have excellent self-healing ability compared with the pure resin coatings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the humid and complex marine environment, metal structures of vessels and all kinds of coastal steel structures become highly susceptible for corrosion. This can lead to problems such as cracking, thinning and perforation of the metal structure, which in turn reduces both the intensity and the lifetime of the metal material. More seriously, if the corrosion situation was not effectively controlled, it would destroy the entire metal structure, resulting in significant property and economic losses [1,2]. Currently, prevention strategies are centered on decreasing the corrosion rate and modifying its mechanism. These strategies include the use of alloy steels [3], the implementation of cathodic as well as anodic protection [4], using surface conservation coatings [5–7], the application on anti-corrosion agents [8] or a combination of these methods [9,10]. Among these, applying protective coatings is one of the best strategies to shield metal surfaces from corrosion. In particular, the use of organic coatings, which effectively protect metal surfaces from corrosive media, is the most common and cost-effective method [11].

Acrylic resin coatings are popular in marine metal protection for their cost-effectiveness, safety, eco-friendliness, and outstanding weather resistance [12–14]. Nevertheless, the free volume and various defects inside the coatings tend to be generated as a result of solvent evaporation during coating and curing [15], leading to some problems such as low permeation resistance with low thermal stability, and these drawbacks extremely limit their application for engineering purposes [16,17]. Among the proposed protection strategies, nanofillers are impressive in enhancing corrosion protection due to their highly specific surfaced area/activity in the coating matrix and very low use content. Nanoparticles such as SiO2 [18], Zn [19], h-BN [20], carbon nanotubes (CNTs) [21], Graphene(G) [22], as well as Graphene Oxide (GO) [23] provide improved barrier performance compared to conventional particles, efficiently fill coating micropores and defects, and extend electrolyte channel length. The fillers mentioned above are not toxic, but the protection of the material can only rely on a physical barrier. Researchers have looked into adding corrosion inhibitors to coatings in an effort to improve their ability to prevent corrosion. Krisada et al. [24] extended the lifetime of the coatings by adding corrosion inhibitors to the coatings and utilizing the released 8HQ to coordinate with the metal ions to reduce the corrosion rate of the coatings. Morozov et al. [25] prepared pH sensitive Ce(DEHP)3 particles and added them to epoxy resins, which greatly improved the protective effect for epoxy coatings. However, the corrosion inhibitors used are difficult to synthesize, costly and toxic, which is not conducive to environmental protection. Given the key role of oxygen in the corrosion process, Cheng et al. [26] proposed an innovative nano-catalyzed corrosion prevention concept. They utilized monoatomic Fe-N-C catalysts to actively adsorb and consume oxygen in the environment, effectively reducing the contribution to corrosion by oxygen and enriching the anti-corrosion qualities of coatings notably. It is necessary to combine the advantages of nanofillers and corrosion inhibitors to prepare a nontoxic nanofiller with both physical barrier and chemical reaction ability.

In recent years, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, high specific area, easy synthesis, and low fabrication cost have attracted much attention [27,28]. Reportedly, incorporating N-CQDs into polymers could effectively resolve defects arising from solvent evaporation during curing, boosting cross-linking density within polymer systems [29,30]. N-CQDs' surface amine, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups formed stable complexes with Fe3+ ions, as elegantly elucidated by Atchudan et al. [31] and Song et al. [32]. It has also been reported that nitrogen doping can induce charge delocalization, regulate the work function of carbon, and increase the catalytic active site of N-CQDs to realize the adsorption and conversion of oxygen [33–35]. The multiple properties of N-CQDs make it become a new type of anticorrosive material with great potential, which provides new ideas for the development of efficient eco-friendly anticorrosive coatings.

In this study, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with special structures (pyridine N, graphite N and pyrrole N, etc.) and abundant functional groups were synthesized via a hydrothermal method with citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursors. Afterwards, N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coatings were prepared by introducing N-CQDs into PAZ/N-PMI resins. Electrochemical tests showed that the composite coating had an impedance modulus as high as 3.88 × 1011 Ω∙cm2, which was four orders of magnitude higher than the 1.67 × 107 Ω∙cm2 of the pure resin, showing excellent corrosion resistance. The findings of the microscopic test indicate that the N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coating has remarkable self-healing performance, with its scratch depth reduced from 36.34 μm to 6.80 μm. Further test analyses, such as X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with linear cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves, showing that the introduction of N-CQDs not only provides plenty of active sites, but also it promotes the conversion of oxygen in the corrosive environment and reduces the erosion of the corrosive medium. In addition, rich functional groups on the surface of N-CQDs can combine with corrosion-generated iron ions to form stable complexes, inhibit the generation of local protocells, and enhance the corrosion resistance of the substrate. Finally, the hydrogen-bonded cross-linked network formed by the introduction of N-CQDs not only enhances the self-healing ability of the coating, but also improves the coating properties, delaying the onset of corrosion. In conclusion, this paper adopts an economical method to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots, which significantly enhances the anticorrosive properties and self-healing ability of acrylic resins, broadens their application in marine engineering, and provides new ideas as well as methods for the development of future high-performance anticorrosive coatings.
Please refer to Supplementary Material for the detail information.

Acrylic resin synthesis carried out in accordance with current practices [36]. N-PMI (22.36 g) was split into two pieces, A and B, with a weight ratio of 1:3 after being dissolved in DMF (60 mL). 25 g of mixed monomer (MMA, Homemade zinc acrylate, BA), 0.75 g of AIBN, 55 g of xylene, and 20 g of n-butanol were combined with Part A. The combination was then put into a flask with three necks and allowed to react for one hour at 85 °C. Then, 2.25 g of AIBN, 75 g of mixed monomer, and Part B were mixed and added dropwise through a constant pressure funnel to a three-necked flask and reacted for three hours. 0.75 g of AIBN was dissolved in a mixture of 20 g of xylene and 15 g of n-butanol and the mixture was added dropwise to a three-necked flask and reacted for 4 h. Finally, the organic solvent was removed by rotary evaporator to obtain acrylic resin (PAZ/N-PMI). See Fig. 1 (a) for synthesis steps.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Earlier experiments [37] were amended somewhat. Firstly, a clear solution was obtained by dissolving 15.72 g of citric acid monohydrate and 4.62 g of ethylenediamine in 150 mL of deionized water and stirring the mixture for 10 mins. The solution was then moved to a high-pressure hydrothermal reactor walled with polytetrafluoroethylene, where it was heated to 200 °C for a continuous 5-hour reaction before the hydrothermal reactor naturally cooled to ambient temperature. Then, the product was filtered and purified using a nylon membrane (0.22 μm) and a dialysis bag (1000 Da). Finally, the product was dried in a vacuum oven for 24 h to obtain N-CQDs powder, and the yield is 10 % (Fig. 1(b)).

First, the Q235 steel substrate was sanded with 600, 800 and 1000 mesh sandpaper to remove the protective film on the substrate surface. Next, the impurities on the substrate surface were treated with a dust-free cloth and alcohol. At the same time, a mixed slurry consisting of N-CQDs and PAZ/N-PMI was prepared and mixed thoroughly. And it was roller coated on the surface of Q235 steel substrate. Finally, the coating was dried in a drying oven for 48 h to obtain a thickness of 150 ± 10 μm (Fig. 1(c)).

Fig. 2 (a) displayed the acrylic resin (PAZ/N-PMI) IR spectral data: The –OH absorption peak was observed at approximately 3430 cm−1. The typical –CH2- asymmetric and symmetric peaks were seen at around 2960 cm−1 and 2880 cm−1, respectively. The bent vibrational peak of –CH2- was observed at approximately 1446 cm−1. The C = O telescopic vibrational peak of carboxylate was observed at approximately 1720 cm−1. The symmetric telescopic vibrational peak of –COO- was observed near 1168 cm−1. Butyl acrylate's –CH3 telescopic vibration spike showed up at 1086 cm−1, whilst the −COOZn + OOC– vibration band peaked at 1602 cm−1 [36]. The unique absorption peaks of C-N were observed at 1360 cm−1 in the N-PMI five-membered ring, while the distinctive absorption peaks of the C = O double bond were observed at 1670 cm−1. The zinc acrylate resin (PAZ/N-PMI) was produced and N-PMI was successfully copolymerized with acrylic monomer, according to the preceding results.

The 1H NMR spectra of acrylic resin (PAZ/N-PMI) were displayed (Fig. 2(d)). The hydrogen peaks of butyl acrylate –CH2- group, methyl methacrylate's –CH3 group and zinc acrylate monomer's –CH3 group were about 3.9 ∼ 4.1 ppm, 3.50 ∼ 3.70 ppm and 2.20 ∼ 2.30 ppm respectively, and N-PMI monomer's phenyl ring's hydrogen peaks were approximately 7.40 ∼ 7.50 ppm. Naturally, the C = C double bond in our N-PMI monomer remained open due to absence of a discernible 2H resonance peak at 6.80 ppm on the 1H NMR spectra. In conclusion, the distinctive peaks in the hydrogen spectra show that PAZ/N-PMI is successfully produced by the polymerization of the four monomers with one another.

The results of the gel chromatographic analysis of the PAZ/N-PMI were presented in Fig. 2(b), and the specific molecular weights are illustrated in Table S1. Showing us a change in polymer's molecular weight after around 6 mins, with its retention duration lasting around 16 mins. The peak molecular weight of the polymer was 9205 g/mol, indicating the synthesized polymer. Please note from Fig. 2(c), the thermal decomposition temperature of PAZ/N-PMI coating to 1 wt% N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coating increases from 345.3 ℃ to 371.9 ℃, which is owing to the formation of non-covalent bond networks in the composite coating, and the destruction of these non-covalent bond networks requires higher energy absorption [38]. PAZ/N-PMI resin is shown in Fig. 2(e) as a clear, transparent orange-brown liquid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N-CQDs are formed by functional group reaction and carbonization in a hydrothermal environment with citric acid along with ethylenediamine as precursors (Fig. 3 (e)). This N-CQDs, about 5–20 nm in size, features a distinct lattice with a spacing of 0.186 nm (Fig. 3(f)). It displays superior fluorescence properties under UV light (365 nm) (Fig. 3(g) and (h)). N-CQDs are formed by FT-IR analysis [32,39,40] revealed the presence of several oxygen and nitrogen functional groups, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino, carbonyl, amide, and epoxy groups, on the surface of N-CQDs (Fig. 3(a)). Kindly find N-CQDs' XPS spectra in Fig. 3(b-d) [41].

Van der Waals and hydrophobic forces are defeated by the significant electrostatic repulsion that N-CQDs possess due to their electronegative groups on the surface. The study found that N-CQDs formed a satisfyingly clear brown translucent dispersion (300 mg/L) post-simple sonication in water with a brilliant Tyndall effect discernible when laser light shone through. N-CQDs' ability to absorb UV light was examined by using a UV spectrophotometer. N-CQDs exhibit two absorption peaks in their UV–visible spectrum (Fig. 7(c)). The absorption peak at 240 nm is caused by the π-π* transition of the aromatic C = C bond, whereas the absorption peak at 340 nm is caused by the n-π* transition of the N-amine-rich functional group on the surface [42].

ZSimpWin software was utilized to fit the EIS data with the similar circuit depicted in Fig. 4 (f-h). The fitting results are shown in Table S2. where Rs represents the solution resistance, Rc represents the coating resistance, Rd represents the diffusion resistance after the erosion of corrosive liquids, Rd1 represents the resistance of corrosive liquids intruding into the higher concentration portion of the corrosive liquids, Rd2 represents the resistance of corrosive liquids intruding into the lower concentration portion of the corrosive liquids, Rct represents the charge transfer resistance, Qc corresponds to the coated constant-phase element, and Qd1 and Qd2 correspond to the double-layer constant-phase element [43,44]. Typically, the impedance modulus at its lowest frequency in the bode plot (aka |Z|0.01Hz) serves as a key reference for evaluating coating corrosion resistance [45].

Nyquist plots were shown for PAZ/N-PMI coatings and composite coatings with N-CQDs additions of 0.5 wt%, 1 wt%, and 2 wt%, respectively, after being bathed in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for one day(Fig. 4(a)). From the figure, Nyquist plots of all the coatings showed only a semicircular impedance arc, which indicates that the coatings are still at the earlier stage of corrosion, where the corrosive substance is kept outside the surface of the coatings, without intruding into the interiors, proving that the coatings have a good protective capacity. Fig. 4(b) illustrates that, following a day of immersion, the |Z|0.01Hz values of N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coatings with varying additions exceed 1011 Ω∙cm2. Of these, the N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coatings containing one weight percent have the highest impedance modulus, measuring |Z|0.01Hz = 3.88 × 1011 Ω∙cm2, which is four orders in magnitude larger than that of the PAZ/N-PMI coating of |Z|0.01Hz = 1.67 × 107 Ω∙cm2 (Fig. S1). Coating resistance is considered to be a key indicator for evaluating the porosity and deterioration of coatings [46], therefore, composite coatings are more resistant to penetration and corrosion than pure resin coatings.

In an attempt to explore the impedance modulus of N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coatings with immersion time more explicitly, please refer to Fig. 4(c-e) for the results of EIS tests on our composite N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI coatings after 59 days of immersion. Specific data are outlined in Table S2. The corrosive medium started to slowly seep into the composite coatings after 24 days of immersion, as evidenced by the rapid contraction of the impedance arcs with varying N-CQDs contents and the obvious separation of the corresponding Bode curves (Fig. S2). On the other hand, the Nyquist plots of the coatings showed only one time constant, suggesting that the corrosive medium only intruded into the coatings and did not penetrate through them to reach the interface between the coatings and the substrates. The 0.5 wt% and 2 % proportional shrinkage are larger, while the 1 wt% proportional shrinkage is relatively small, and the 1 wt% N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coating still has the highest impedance modulus of |Z|0.01Hz = (8.70 × 1010 Ω∙cm2) after immersion for 24 days, which indicates that the coating with the content of 1 wt% is the most stable and best in resisting erosion. After continued immersion in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution for 59 days, the |Z|0.01Hz value of 2 wt% N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coatings appeared to be greatly reduced, with three time constants, the substrate's surface has been infiltrated by the corrosive medium, and corrosion was carried out in the middle and late stages. At the same time, 0.5 wt% and 1 wt% said that they also began to appear in two time constants, which indicated that the corrosive medium's further erosion. 0.5 wt% and 1 wt% began to enter the middle stage of corrosion. Among these, 1 wt% still has an impedance modulus of 8.78 × 109 Ω∙cm2 or more, and the coating did not fail, providing long-term corrosion protection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research on multiple anti-corrosion mechanisms of composite coatings
Synergistic anti-corrosion mechanism of PAZ/N-PMI resin and N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the test results for PAZ/N-PMI coating surface morphology (Fig. 5 (a1)) and cross-sectional profiles (Fig. 5(a2)). Wherein the cross-section of the coating exhibits an increased number of tiny cracks and holes. These features are likely the result of an irregularity in the rate at which the organic solvents volatilize and the rate at which the film-forming resins shrink during the coating curing process. These minuscule cracks and holes might disrupt the integrity of the coatings as they enable corrosive substances to penetrate the substrate via such pores. By contrast, the surface and cross profile of N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coating is smooth, with few defects, and the cross-section is very dense, with only a small number of pores (Fig. 5(b1 and b2)). This excellent film-forming ability of the composite coatings can be attributed to the fact that the hydroxyl, amino, carboxyl, and epoxy groups on the surface of the N-CQDs can form hydrogen-bonded cross-links with the acrylic resins and neighboring N-CQDs, which act as a similar cross-linking agent, increasing the compatibility between resins and between resins and fillers. In addition, due to the aromatic heterocyclic structure of N-CQDs, van der Waals bonding also exists between neighboring N-CQDs, which further enhances the cross-linking effect [29]. The synergistic effect of N-CQDs and PAZ/N-PMI resulted in the composite coatings with excellent film-forming properties, which provided an important contribution for the coatings possessing excellent corrosion protection. The anticorrosive mechanism is showed in Fig. 5(c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical anti-corrosion mechanism of composite coating
ce:section.ce:para: Zhu et al. [29] reported that carbon dots synthesized in different ways have the ability to catalyze oxygen, giving an inspiration to this research work that N-CQDs in coatings can improve the corrosion resistance of coatings by consuming corrosive mediators (oxygen) in addition to constructing crosslinked networks, which can be used to protect the steel plates. Characterization of N-CQDs using linear cyclic voltammetry (CV) (Fig. 7(b)). In the blank sample without the passage of oxygen (the blank sample contains dissolved oxygen at room temperature and pressure), it can be found that there is a relatively obvious reduction peak on the reduction path on the CV curve, indicating that a redox reaction has occurred; then measure the CV curve in the saturated oxygen state, and it is obviously found that the reduction peak becomes larger, indicating that with the increase of O2 content, the strength of the redox becomes larger, which proves that the N-CQDs have a better catalytic effect on O2. Referring to Fig. 7(a) for the anti-corrosion mechanism, when oxygen, water and other media invade the coating, passing through the nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots embedded in the coating, the O2 redox reaction occurs on the N-CQDs, reducing the corrosion medium levels, so the erosion can't proceed well, which enhances the anticorrosive ability of the resin coating. The catalytic performance of N-CQDs can be attributed to the doping of elemental N (Fig. 3(c)). The pyridine N, graphite N and pyrrole N in the structure of N-CQDs play a significant role during the catalytic process.

Fig. 6
is an analysis of the transformation effect of O2 in N-CQDs, we were able to find that the number of electron transfers catalyzed by N-CQDs is between 2 and 4, a finding that reveals the possible simultaneous existence of both two-electron and four-electron transfer pathways in the oxygen conversion process(Fig. 6(a-c)). In the two-electron transfer pathway, the oxygen molecule first undergoes an initial reduction process to hydrogen peroxide as an intermediate product. Subsequently, hydrogen peroxide accepts further electrons and is eventually reduced to water. In contrast, the four-electron transfer path is more direct, allowing oxygen molecules to accept four electrons at once and convert directly to water without intermediate steps [47,48]. The specific steps are as follows:
(1) Two-electron transfer path: O2 + 2H+ + 2e- → H2O2
(2) H2O2 + 2H+ + 2e- → H2O
(3) Four-electron transfer path:O2 + 4H+ + 4e- → H2O

Overall, although the two-electron and four-electron paths do not have the same rate of converting O2 to generate H2O, the most general purpose is the same, which achieves the purpose of corrosive medium (O2) depletion and enhances the anticorrosive performance. In order to deeply investigate the specific effects of the intermediate product (H₂O₂) produced during oxygen conversion on metal corrosion, we carefully designed the following experiments. First, we configured two sets of solutions: one was a simple 3.5 % NaCl solution, and the other was a mixed solution to which N-CQDs (at a concentration of 400 mg/L) were added. Then, we passed oxygen into these two sets of solutions until the solutions were saturated with oxygen. Subsequently, we immersed the well-polished Q235 steel sheets into each of these two sets of solutions for 6 h and observed their corrosion under a light microscope. The experimental results showed that in the 3.5 % NaCl solution without N-CQDs, the surface of Q235 steel suffered from severe corrosion, and obvious, deep and large corrosion pits appeared (Fig. 6(d-f)). However, in the mixed solution containing N-CQDs, the surface of Q235 steel was significantly protected with only a few shallow corrosion pits (Fig. 6(g-i)). This experimental result shows that N-CQDs do not only effectively protect the metal from corrosion, but also they play an excellent protective role even when intermediate products such as H₂O₂ are produced in a highly concentrated O2 environment.

The abundant and varied functional groups (amine, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups) on N-CQDs surfaces, which can combine with Fe3+ ions to form stable N-CQDs@Fe3+ complexes (Fig. 7(a)). N-CQDs have been the subject of much research [28,49]. Upon the introduction of aqueous Fe3+ solution formed by dissolving ferric(III) chloride hexahydrate into the N-CQDs sample, the fluorescence of the N-CQDs is quenched due to electron/charge interactions [50] (Fig. 7(e)). Comparison of FT-IR spectra and UV spectra after the addition of Fe3+ to the N-CQDs solution revealed that there was a huge change in the plots after the addition of Fe3+ (Fig. 7(c) and (d)), indicating that Fe3+ ions were coordinated with the functional groups on the surface of the N-CQDs [50,51].

Fe corrosion products formed by Q235 steel in the oxygen-containing environment are mostly existing in Fe3+. The Fe3+ and other ions in the environment will act as the electrolyte, increase the transfer of electrons in solution, and then promote the corrosion continues to occur. N-CQDs can form stable complexes with the generated Fe3+, which consumes Fe3+, reduces the ionic concentration in the local electrolyte, so that the primary cell can not be formed well, achieving the protective effect. At the same time, the resulting complexes adsorption on the surface of the steel plate, creating a robust local protective film [52], which restricts the transmission of corrosive media (H2O, O2, and Cl-) in the plate [53], and thus reduces the corrosion rate of the steel plate. After stripping the coating, it was found that no N-element dispersion of N-CQDs was detected at the interface between the pure PAZ/N-PMI resin and the substrate ( Fig. S3); whereas, N-element dispersion of N-CQDs was detected at the interface between the N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI resin and the substrate ( Fig. S4). The above results indicate that N-CQDs are uniformly dispersed at the interface between N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI resin and the substrate. Fig. 7(g) shows the EIS maps of Fe3+ solution and N-CQDs@Fe3+ solution, which were fitted according to the circuit diagram of Fig. 4(f), and the fitted data is shown in Table S3. It was found that the resistance of the solution with the addition of N-CQDs increased at the same concentration of Fe3+, which suggests that the formation of the complexes between the N-CQDs and Fe3+ inhibits the generation of the primary cell.

To delve deeper into the prevention mechanisms against interfacial corrosion, we observed changes in the potential polarization curve dynamics of Q235 steel after 24-hour immersion in 3.5 % NaCl solutions with or without 300 mg/L N-CQDs. Evaluations on critical corrosion factors like Ecorr , icorr , βa , βc were performed via Tafel extrapolation (Table S4) and among these, inhibition efficiency η was tallied according to:
(4)

We define icorr,0 representing the corrosion current without N-CQDs, and icorr,1 the one with. Observing Fig. 7(f), we find that the corrosion current icorr decreases dramatically after the introduction of N-CQDs. Specifically, it decreased from 3.06196 × 10-6 A cm−2 to 1.94178 × 10-6 A cm−2, indicating a decrease in charge transfer. In addition, after immersing in N-CQDs solution for 24 h, the icorr value of Q235 steel decreased sharply from 5.01765 × 10-6 to 1.33506 × 10-6 A cm−2, with a high inhibition rate of 73.4 %. The high inhibition efficiency of N-CQDs is attributed to the fact that heteroatoms (N and O) with free electrons in N-CQDs interact with the unoccupied 3d orbitals of Fe3+ ions and adsorb on the steel surface, thus preventing the transfer of corrosive media [53]. Indeed, our tests showed that steel plates steeped in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution with 300 mg/L N-CQDs enjoyed enhanced protection (Figs. S5(a1-b2)). This affirms our earlier conclusions.

The scratch layers' corrosion resilience was assessed via a neutral salt spray test (Fig. 8 ). It can be seen that after 6 days of salt spray erosion in the confined space, the “X” scratches on the surface of the 0.5 wt%, 1 wt% and 2 wt% coatings showed significant healing, and there was no peeling of the coating and no corrosion in the localized areas of the “X” scratches. This is due to the restorative properties of the coating and the fact that the restoration partially blocks the salt spray. Comparatively, PAZ/N-PMI coating showed initial healing tendencies at the “X” scratch, yet rust formation occurred. This might be due to the coating's own repair mechanism, albeit at a slower rate and less effectively, failing to sufficiently shield from salt mist, leading to corrosion. Furthermore, these coatings displayed varying shades and glossiness which could be linked to the PAZ/N-PMI resin engaging in ionic exchange reactions with NaCl solution in salt mist. Following ten days of exposure to salt mist, “X” scratches on the surface of the composite coating with 0.5 wt%, 1 wt% and 2 wt% of N-CQDs were notably minimized, virtually invisible, with evident self-healing features, demonstrating exceptional healing capabilities. The PAZ/N-PMI coating surface, on the other hand, showed more holes and rust with more severe corrosion. The appearance of these rusts and holes further illustrates the poor corrosion resistance and repair performance of the PAZ/N-PMI coating, which is unable to heal the damage and shield the penetration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of self-healing properties of coatings
ce:section.ce:para: After scratching, the self-healing properties of pure PAZ/N-PMI resin coatings and composite coatings with the addition of 1 wt% N-CQDs were tested by using metallographic microscopy (1 % demonstrated the best self-healing ability, as detailed in Fig. S6). A knife crafted an “X” on the coating's surface. Then it was gently immersed in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution and maintained at a cozy 40 °C. An optical microscope was employed, delivering revealing results as seen in Fig. 9 . PAZ/N-PMI resin in the soaking 18 h, the coating “X” edge has a tendency to converge to the middle, but the overall repair effect is not obvious and slow. After 32 h of soaking, scratches on the PAZ/N-PMI resin coating have the tendency to converge more to the middle. But only a small part of the coating repairs, and the repair effect is poor. To achieve the target repair effect, it must extend the healing time (Fig. 9(a1-a3)). In contrast, the “X” scratches on the surface of the 1 wt% N-CQDs composite coating began to close slowly after 18 h of immersion, and it can be seen that the “X” scratches have become significantly lighter. After 32 h of immersion, the “X” scratches on the 1 wt% N-CQDs composite coating were basically invisible, and the repair effect was remarkable, which indicated that the addition of N-CQDs significantly enhanced the self-repairing ability of the composite coating (Fig. 9(b1-b3)).

To evaluate the potential repair efficiency of the coatings, we gently exposed 1 wt% N-CQDs composites and pure PAZ/N-PMI samples to 3.5 wt% NaCl solution and had kept them at 40 ℃ for 20 h. The test procedure is shown in Fig. 10 . From the three-dimensional morphology, it is evident that the scratches of N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coating are obviously smaller, and the depth of the scratches is reduced from 36.34 μm to 6.80 μm, and the depth of the healing is 29.54 μm ( Fig. S7(a)); in contrast, the three-dimensional morphology of the PAZ/N-PMI coating has less change, and the depth of the scratches is reduced from 44.05 to 25.49 μm, and the depth of the healing is just 18.56 μm (Fig. S7(b)). Therefore, the N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coating has excellent repair performance contrasted with the PAZ/N-PMI coating.

Fig. 10(c) depicts the coating self-healing mechanism. After the blade had scraped the N-PMI coating, its coating fracture surface was tightly squeezed by the side, which prompted the movement and entanglement of the PAZ/N-PMI polymer molecular chain segments toward the scratched area of the coating to realize the repair of the resin. Therefore, the self-healing mechanism of PAZ/N-PMI coating is the movement and entanglement of polymer molecular chain segments [54]. In addition to the movement and entanglement of molecular chain segments in the N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coating, the N-CQDs nanoparticles indicate that they contain a large many of reactive groups (–OH, –NH2, –COOH, etc.) that can form hydrogen bonding with O, N, and H on the PAZ/N-PMI polymers, which leads to the formation of a rich hydrogen-bonded cross-linking network in the composite coating; It's widely believed that the rich reversible hydrogen bonding can endow nanomaterials with potential self-healing properties [38,55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adhesion ability, impact resistance, hardness, tensile properties and self-healing efficiency of coatings
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the firmness of the adhesion between the coating and the substrate, a series of adhesion tests were conducted [56,57]. Given that a strong adhesion is essential for the coating to protect the substrate, in this study we used the circle test, the cross-cut test, and the pull-out test to carefully evaluate the adhesion between the prepared coating and the substrate. In the scribing experiments, we observed the degree of coating integrity within the scribing range of the circular roller line and expressed the adhesion of the coatings in terms of a specific level. As shown in Fig. 11 (a) and (b), the PAZ/N-PMI coatings showed poor adhesion as there was more peeling after scratching the circles. In contrast, the N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coating shows only a very small amount of coating shedding, with most of the area remaining intact, and the test level is stable at 1–2, so it can be judged that the N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coating has excellent adhesion. In the cross-cut tests, the coating flaking rate becomes a key indicator of the strength of adhesion between the coating and the substrate. As shown in Fig. 11(c) and (d), the PAZ/N-PMI coating showed coating flaking at the edges of its square lattice and in the square area, while the N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coating, did not show any coating flaking at the edges of the square lattice and in the square area, which further confirmed the good adhesion of the composite coating to the substrate. In order to quantify the adhesion of the coatings more accurately, a pull-off test was conducted. The test results show that the bonding strength of N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coating is as high as 1.65 MPa, while the bonding strength of PAZ/N-PMI coating is only 1.05 MPa. This data clearly shows that the introduction of N-CQDs effectively enhanced the adhesion of PAZ/N-PMI coatings by as much as 57.1 %. The significant enhancement of the adhesion can be attributed to the effective coordination between the functional groups contained in the N-CQDs and the iron, which can be firmly adsorbed on the steel surface (Adhesion enhancement can be seen in Fig. S8), thus enhancing the mechanical properties of the coating [58]. To further verify the mechanical properties of the coatings, impact experiments were conducted. As shown in Fig. 11(e) and (f), at an impact height of 60 cm, obvious spalling occurred around the PAZ/N-PMI coated impact sample, indicating its poor mechanical properties. On the other hand, there was no spalling around the impact sample of the N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coating, which fully proves that the N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coating has more excellent mechanical properties compared with the pure PAZ/N-PMI coating.

In order to evaluate the self-healing ability of the composites in depth, we tested the tensile properties of the samples before and after self-healing [59–62]. First, in the tensile test, the pure PAZ/N-PMI coating exhibited a strain of 75.8 % and a stress of 1.0 MPa (Fig. 11(g)). However, when N-CQDs is introduced into the coating, the strain of N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coating is significantly increased to 322.2 %, and the stress is also increased to 1.6 MPa (Fig. 11(j)). This data clearly shows that N-CQDs plays a positive role in improving the mechanical properties of the coating. To further verify the self-healing ability of the coatings, we repaired the pure PAZ/N-PMI coating and the N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coating by heating at 60 °C for 45 mins. The performance test results after repair showed that the strain of the pure PAZ/N-PMI coating was only recovered to 27.6 % and the self-healing efficiency was only 36.4 % (Fig. 11(g-i)). This result reflects the relatively weak self-healing ability of the pure coating. In contrast, the N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI composite coating exhibited excellent self-healing performance under the same repair conditions. Its strain recovery was up to 290.3 % and its self-healing efficiency was up to 90.1 %. More importantly, the self-healing efficiency of the composite coating remained at 72.6 % after four repairs (Fig. 11(j-l)). This remarkable healing efficiency is mainly attributed to the dynamic changes of hydrogen bonding in the N-CQDs@PAZ/N-PMI coating. Under heating conditions, the hydrogen bonds in the coatings were opened, which promoted the chain segment movement and allowed the coatings to self-healing. When the temperature returns to room temperature, hydrogen bonds are formed again, maintaining the structure and properties of the coating. The incorporation of N-CQDs do not only enhance the mechanical properties of the coatings, but also they facilitate the dynamic change of hydrogen bonds in the coatings through its special structure and properties, thus endowing the coatings with excellent self-healing properties.

In this study, we prepared acrylic resin, nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and their composite coatings, which have excellent anti-corrosion ability, with an impedance modulus of 1011 Ω∙cm2, and after up to 59 days of corrosion, there is still 107 Ω∙cm2 impedance modulus, which is able to provide long-term and stable protection for the substrate. The excellent anti-corrosion performance of the composite coating depends on the result of the interaction of PAZ/N-PMI resin with N-CQDs, and has three anti-corrosion functions:
(1)
N-CQDs form a rich non-covalent crosslinking network with PAZ/N-PMI resin, which improves the film-forming properties of the coating.
(2)
N-CQDs catalyzing consumes oxygen, reducing the corrosive medium erosion.
(3)
N-CQDs and Fe3+ formative of stabilized complexes slows down the continuous corrosion of the substrate interface.

At the same time, the rich cross-linking network between carbon quantum dots and acrylic resin provides conditions for fracture repair, giving it excellent self-healing properties, greatly extending the life of acrylic resin, so that acrylic resin can serve better in the process of use.
